subtitle: From Blood-Soaked Victory to the Dharma of a King

panels.shot: low-angle wide of a lone emperor on a rocky ridge above a vast, scarred land
panels.action: Emperor Ashoka stands in armor and heavy cloak on a wind-swept height, gazing over a hazy landscape of distant ruins and smoking fields that hint at a recent war, his face a mix of power and burden.

dialogue.text: Ashoka, heir of Chandragupta, conqueror of Kalinga, would carve his own heart on stone and turn an empire from the path of the sword to the path he called dhamma.

i18n.hi: चंद्रगुप्त का वंशज, कलिंग विजेता अशोक, अपना मन पत्थरों पर उकेरकर साम्राज्य को तलवार से धर्म की ओर मोड़ देता है।

panels.shot: epic bird's-eye over a sprawling Mauryan camp before battle
panels.action: Emperor Ashoka, in full martial regalia, surveys from horseback a vast Mauryan army massed near a wide river, lines of war elephants and infantry stretching toward the wooded hills of Kalinga.

dialogue.text: Around 261 BCE, the Mauryan realm towered over the subcontinent. Only Kalinga, rich and proud on the eastern coast, still stood outside the shadow of Pataliputra.

i18n.hi: लगभग 261 ईसा पूर्व, मौर्य साम्राज्य पूरे उपमहाद्वीप पर छाया था। सिर्फ पूर्वी तट पर समृद्ध, गर्वीला कलिंग ही पाटलिपुत्र की छाया से बाहर था।

panels.shot: medium close-up profile of Ashoka on horseback, eyes narrowed toward distant Kalinga fortifications
panels.action: Ashoka sits rigid on his horse, jaw set, banners snapping behind him, as he stares toward distant city walls rising beyond the river and forest, his expression fierce and unyielding.

dialogue.speaker: emperor_ashoka
dialogue.text: Kalinga must be brought under order. The empire must be whole.

i18n.hi: कलिंग को अनुशासन में लाना होगा। साम्राज्य को पूरा होना है।

panels.shot: wide, slightly elevated view of the battlefield after the clash, smoke hanging over broken formations
panels.action: On a trampled field by the river, battle formations lie shattered; elephants stand restless, broken chariots tilt at odd angles, and distant plumes of dust drift where fighters have withdrawn.

dialogue.text: The clash with Kalinga is not described by Ashoka blow by blow. His stones speak only of the outcome: a kingdom crushed, many thousands taken, and the land subdued.

i18n.hi: कलिंग की लड़ाई अशोक ने वार‑वार नहीं लिखी। उसके शिलालेख बस नतीजा कहते हैं: राज्य कुचला गया, हजारों बंदी बने, धरती झुक गई।

panels.shot: long shot of Ashoka on a ridge above the battlefield, small against a vast, quiet devastation
panels.action: Ashoka stands apart on a rocky slope, his armor dulled with dust, gazing over a wide plain littered with abandoned gear and silent shapes, the river glinting dully beyond.

dialogue.text: Later, in his thirteenth Rock Edict, Ashoka would recall this view, not as a glory, but as a wound: "One hundred and fifty thousand were deported; many hundreds of thousands died."

i18n.hi: बाद में, तेरहवें शिलालेख में, अशोक इस दृश्य को महिमा नहीं, घाव की तरह याद करता है: "डेढ़ लाख निर्वासित हुए; कई लाख मारे गए।"

panels.shot: close-up on Ashoka's face as he looks downward, eyes shadowed, the battlefield reflected faintly
panels.action: Ashoka's face fills the frame, sweat and dust streaking his brow, his eyes dark and troubled as he looks down, the ruined field faintly mirrored in his gaze.

dialogue.text: He names no enemies, no heroes. Instead he records his own heart: grief, deepening. The conqueror begins to see not just captured land, but shattered families.

i18n.hi: वह न दुश्मन का नाम लेता है, न वीरों का। वह बस अपना दिल लिखता है: शोक, जो गहराता जाता है। विजेता को अब सिर्फ जीती जमीन नहीं, टूटे घर दिखाई देते हैं।

panels.shot: medium shot of Ashoka walking slowly among survivors and prisoners, flanked by silent guards
panels.action: Ashoka moves on foot through the aftermath, his helmet now in his hand, as weary prisoners sit in clusters and women and children cling to one another, guards standing watch in uneasy silence.

dialogue.speaker: emperor_ashoka
dialogue.text: If this is victory, what is defeat?

i18n.hi: अगर यह जीत है, तो हार कैसी होगी?

panels.shot: intimate mid-close of Ashoka as a Kalinga elder kneels before him, off-frame, only a frail hand visible
panels.action: Ashoka pauses as a frail hand reaches toward him from below, his shoulders slightly hunched, the faint lines of guilt etched around his mouth, the camp and ruined land blurred behind.

dialogue.text: The edict does not name this moment, yet its tone suggests such encounters. For he writes of "the greatest sorrow" falling not only on the conquered, but on the victor who caused it.

i18n.hi: शिलालेख इस घड़ी का नाम नहीं लेता, पर उसका स्वर ऐसे ही प्रसंगों का संकेत देता है। वह लिखता है कि "सबसे बड़ा दुख" सिर्फ हारे पर नहीं, बल्कि उस विजेता पर भी गिरता है जिसने यह किया।

panels.shot: low-angle medium shot of Ashoka back in a Mauryan hall, armor set aside, seated heavily on the throne
panels.action: In a grand but dim hall at Pataliputra, Ashoka sits slumped on a carved throne, armor removed, simple cloth beneath, his hands resting uncertainly on the lion-armed seat.

dialogue.text: Back in the capital, the emperor who had strengthened the Mauryan machine faced a new adversary: his own conscience. Kalinga had fallen, yet the victory did not sit easily upon him.

i18n.hi: राजधानी लौटकर, जिसने मौर्य सत्ता को तेज किया था, वही सम्राट अब नए शत्रु से भिड़ा: अपने ही अंतरात्मा से। कलिंग गिर चुका था, पर यह जीत उसके मन पर भारी थी।

panels.shot: slow push-in on Ashoka seated cross-legged in a quiet chamber, a lamp beside him, scrolls or palm leaves nearby
panels.action: Ashoka sits cross-legged on a simple mat in a quiet chamber, hands folded, a single oil lamp casting soft light over palm-leaf documents and a neatly rolled royal seal beside him.

dialogue.text: By this time, he had already encountered Buddhist teachings. The edicts hint at influences from many traditions, yet show a ruler turning inward, weighing policy against suffering.

i18n.hi: तब तक वह बौद्ध उपदेशों से मिल चुका था। शिलालेख कई परंपराओं के असर का संकेत देते हैं, पर दिखाते हैं एक राजा को, जो भीतर झाँक रहा है, नीति को दुख के तराजू पर तौल रहा है।

panels.shot: medium wide of Ashoka standing before a bare rock face, sculptors and scribes around him, early morning light
panels.action: At the foot of a large, smooth rock, Ashoka stands with a firm yet grave expression, gesturing as artisans and scribes attend, one measuring the stone while another sits ready with stylus and palm leaf.

dialogue.speaker: emperor_ashoka
dialogue.text: Let this be cut into stone. Let all hear what their king has learned.

i18n.hi: इसे पत्थर पर काटो। सब जान लें, उनके राजा ने क्या समझा है।

panels.shot: close-up of chisel striking rock, with Ashoka's silhouette faint in the background
panels.action: A mason's chisel bites into the rock surface, tiny chips flying, while Ashoka's faint silhouette stands behind, hands clasped, as the first lines of his edict begin to take form.

dialogue.text: In his Major Rock Edict XIII, he orders his remorse proclaimed: that the slaughter and deportation in Kalinga "are considered very grievous" to Devanampriya, the Beloved of the Gods.

i18n.hi: महाशिलालेख १३ में वह अपने पछतावे की घोषणा कराता है: कि कलिंग में हुआ कत्ल और निर्वासन देवप्रिय, देवताओं के प्रिय, को "अत्यंत दुःखद" लगता है।

panels.shot: medium shot of Ashoka reading back a draft to scribes in a shaded grove near the rock
panels.action: Ashoka stands beneath a tree, holding a palm leaf as scribes crouch nearby, listening intently, the carved rock visible behind them, half-shadowed and half in sunlight.

dialogue.speaker: emperor_ashoka
dialogue.text: No conquest gives him such joy as conquest by dhamma.

i18n.hi: उसे अब कोई विजय उतनी प्रिय नहीं, जितनी धर्म की विजय।

panels.shot: wide shot of the Dhauli hill area with the rock edict, Ashoka visiting Kalinga with a smaller retinue
panels.action: In the hilly countryside of Kalinga, Ashoka, now dressed more simply, approaches a rock face with freshly carved inscriptions, accompanied by a modest retinue of officials and attendants.

dialogue.text: At Dhauli and Jaugada he had separate edicts cut, addressed to his officers in Kalinga, binding his remorse to policy, turning feeling into instructions for rule.

i18n.hi: धौली और जौगड़ा में उसने अलग शिलालेख खुदवाए, कलिंग के अधिकारियों के नाम, जहाँ उसका पछतावा नीति बन गया, भावना शासन के नियम।

panels.shot: medium shot of Ashoka addressing a semi-circle of officials in Kalinga, gesture open yet firm
panels.action: Ashoka stands before a semi-circle of Kalinga and Mauryan officials, his right hand lifted in an open, calming gesture, while the newly carved rock stands nearby like a silent witness.

dialogue.speaker: emperor_ashoka
dialogue.text: All men are my children. You are to ensure their welfare and justice.

i18n.hi: सब लोग मेरे बच्चे हैं। तुम उनका भला और न्याय सुनिश्चित करोगे।

panels.shot: close-up on Ashoka’s hand placing a sealed order into an officer’s hands, with villagers visible at a distance
panels.action: Ashoka solemnly hands a sealed order to a regional officer, their hands meeting in the foreground while, in the distance, villagers watch cautiously from the edge of a field.

dialogue.text: These Kalinga edicts speak of fairness in punishment, patience with people, the duty of officials to move among their charges, to report, to correct, to protect.

i18n.hi: ये कलिंग शिलालेख दंड में न्याय, प्रजा के साथ धैर्य, और अधिकारियों के कर्तव्य की बात करते हैं—कि वे लोगों के बीच घूमें, देखें, बताएं, सुधारें, सुरक्षा दें।

panels.shot: wide shot of Ashoka’s envoys departing on roads, ships, and forest paths from a central hub
panels.action: From a bustling way-station, Ashoka's messengers fan out on roads, riverboats, and forest paths, each bearing rolled edicts, as the emperor watches from a balcony above the gate.

dialogue.text: Ashoka ordered his message carved and carried across his dominions and beyond, to frontier chiefs and neighboring rulers, seeking not more territory, but regard for his dhamma.

i18n.hi: अशोक ने अपना संदेश पूरे राज्य में और सीमाओं के पार खुदवाकर भिजवाया, सीमा‑सरदारों और पड़ोसी राजाओं तक, अब और जमीन नहीं, बल्कि अपने dhamma का सम्मान खोजते हुए।

panels.shot: medium shot of an older Ashoka standing by a pillar, hand resting on its polished surface, city life behind him
panels.action: Years later, an older Ashoka in plain royal robes stands beside a polished stone pillar, resting his hand upon it, while in the background, people of different groups move peacefully through the city.

dialogue.text: Later Buddhist traditions would make him a saint-king, polishing the tale. The stones themselves show a human ruler, flawed yet altering the very purpose of his power.

i18n.hi: बाद की बौद्ध कथाएँ उसे संत‑राजा बना कर कहानी चमकाती हैं। पर पत्थर दिखाते हैं एक इंसान शासक को, जो अपूर्ण है, फिर भी अपनी शक्ति के मकसद को बदल देता है।

panels.shot: high, contemplative wide of the rock edicts and pillars enduring in a quiet landscape centuries later
panels.action: Centuries later in the scene, Ashoka is absent; only his rock with inscriptions and a lone pillar stand weathered in a quiet landscape, as travelers pass by and pause to trace the ancient carvings.

dialogue.text: In choosing to admit the hurt he caused, Ashoka set a rare mark in political history: a conqueror who declared remorse and sought to rule, thereafter, by conscience as well as by law.

i18n.hi: अपने किए हुए घाव को मानकर, अशोक ने राजनीति में दुर्लभ मिसाल रखी: एक विजेता, जिसने खुलेआम पछतावा लिखा और ठान लिया कि आगे का राज, कानून के साथ‑साथ अंतरात्मा से भी चलेगा।

subtitle_i18n.en: From Blood-Soaked Victory to the Dharma of a King
subtitle_i18n.hi: खून भरी जीत से राजधर्म तक
